q: Stimulus is a detectable change in the internal or external environment which influence a temporary increase of physiological activity.
Coordination in plants is undertaken by a nervous system like animals.
The responses to external stimuli produced by the working of various organs in a systematic and controlled manner are known as coordination.
The movement of plant parts is due only to external stimuli such as light intensity and duration.
Phytohormones are chemical signals that plants use to sense things around them and respond to them.
Plant responses have evolved because they provide the plant with some type of selective disadvantage in their environment.
The environmental stimuli to which plants respond include both abiotic and biotic stress.
Responses to herbivory are classified as responses to abiotic stress.
Plants lack a nervous system but can still respond to their environment in various ways.
External stimuli include water, gravity, and chemicals but exclude temperature and touch.
The process of development occurs in an ordered fashion within a single diploid cell called zygote.
Growth can be defined as a reversible increase in size of an individual cell or organ.
The meristematic phase of growth occurs in regions such as the shoot apex and root apex.
Cells of the meristems are isodiametric and consist of a small nucleus and sparse cytoplasm.
Cells in the elongation phase of growth are highly vacuolated and found around the meristematic cells.
The maturation phase of growth is primarily characterized by the rapid enlargement of cells.
Optimum temperature for plant growth is $20-35^{\circ}C$.
Genetic factors are not involved in the overall maintenance of a plant during its growth phases.
Water is essential for maintaining the turgidity of plants and for enzymatic activities in the protoplasm.
Indole acetic acid (IAA) is a strong organic acid produced naturally by plants as the first growth regulator.
Phytohormones are essential organic compounds required by plants in very small quantities.
A single plant hormone may only regulate one specific aspect of plant growth and development.
Auxin stimulates the elongation of cells in the shoot and promotes cell enlargement.
In younger parts of a plant, auxin induces the process of abscission in leaves and fruits.
Gibberellins are plant hormones that include gibberellic acid and are produced in young organs like apical leaves.
Gibberellins are transported in plants exclusively by diffusion through the xylem.
Cytokinin is a basic hormone and purine derivative that primarily acts on cell division.
Coconut milk is a rich source of gibberellins used to stimulate stem elongation.
Ethylene is the only gaseous phytohormone and is produced naturally from the amino acid methionine.
Maximum synthesis of ethylene occurs during the early growth of internodes and leaf bases.
Abscisic acid is known as stress hormone and is produced naturally when a plant is exposed to stress.
Abscisic acid acts as a synergist to all growth promoters to enhance plant development.
Seed dormancy is the internal inhibition of germination of a normal seed even under most favourable conditions.
In epigeal germination, the epicotyl elongates faster than the hypocotyl to push the cotyledons above ground.
Hypogeal germination is a type where the epicotyl elongates faster than the hypocotyl.
Vivipary refers to the germination of seeds after they have fallen from the parent tree into saline soils.
Short day plants require a shorter photoperiod than their critical photoperiod in order to initiate flowering.
Florigen is a hormone synthesized in the roots that is responsible for the initiation of flowering in leaves.
Photomorphogenesis involves the inhibition of stem elongation and the differentiation of chloroplasts.
Phytochrome is a red-green pigment containing a covalently attached chromophore.
Light is perceived in leaves by photoreceptors such as phytochrome, cryptochrome and phototropin.
Day neutral plants initiate flowering only when subjected to a specific duration of photoperiodism.
Phototropic responses of seedlings and mature plant leaves are considered morphogenic responses to light.
Seed germination in light demonstrates that all seeds require darkness to grow effectively.
Phytochrome is a blue-green pigment protein that allows plants to sense light direction and quality.
Vernalization is a phenomenon related to the response of plants to the quality of light intensity.
Growth regulators are manufactured by living protoplasm within the plant body.
Mineral nutrition acts as a source of energy but cannot be used as raw material for synthesis of protoplasm.
Gibberellins stimulate the synthesis of hydrolytic enzymes like amylases and proteases during seed germination.
Cytokinins induce flowering in plants and inhibit the development of the chloroplast.
Coordination is the process involved in the detection of stimuli by receptors and the integration of information received.
The coordination process starts with the response and ends with the stimulus from the environment.
Living organisms respond to changes in the internal environment and the external environment to ensure continuity of life.
The human nervous system is made up exclusively of the central nervous system located in the brain.
The central nervous system is composed of the brain and the spinal cord located at the central axis of the body.
The forebrain comprises the cerebellum, pons, and medulla oblongata.
The cerebrum is the largest part of the brain and accounts for more than four fifths of the total weight of the brain.
Thalamus acts as the great relay station and provides connections for the sense of smell.
Hypothalamus helps to regulate the body's internal environment and basic drives such as thirst and hunger.
The midbrain is located above the pons and plays an important role in the control of smooth muscles.
Cerebellum is involved in the unconscious coordination of posture, reflexes, and body movements.
The outer white matter of the spinal cord consists of cell bodies and dendrites of many spinal neurones.
The spinal column consists of a series of backbones called vertebrae which protect the delicate tissues of the spinal cord.
The somatic nervous system is under involuntary control and services the internal organs.
Cranial nerves consist of 12 pairs and are largely associated with functions in the head, neck, and face.
The autonomic nervous system is under conscious control and regulates internal reactions to situations.
Reflex arcs are simple connections of only three neurones used to transmit messages rapidly.
The sympathetic nervous system is typically activated when the body is calm and at rest.
Sympathetic neurones release a neurotransmitter called norepinephrine which has an excitatory effect on target muscles.
The parasympathetic system uses norepinephrine to control organ responses during the rest-and-digest phase.
Neurones are the basic structural and functional units of the nervous system specialized to conduct electrochemical signals.
Glial cells are a type of neurone that conducts impulses away from the cell body to adjacent muscles.
Dendrites are short, branching terminals of dendron that receive nerve impulses and relay them to the cell body.
The axon conducts impulses toward the cell body and ranges in length from $1 mm$ to $1 m$.
The myelin sheath is a fatty, insulating layer made up of Schwann cells that wrap themselves around the axon.
Sensory neurones transmit impulses from the central nervous system to the sensory receptors in the skin.
Interneurones are found entirely within the central nervous system and act as a link between sensory and motor neurones.
Bipolar neurones have several dendrites and a single axon and are found in the brain and spinal cord.
The connection between two neurones, or a neurone and an effector, is called a synapse.
The synaptic cleft is a small gap between neurones that is about $2 \mu m$ wide.
Synaptic vesicles release their contents into the synaptic cleft by the process of exocytosis.
Neurotransmitters diffuse across the synapse taking about $5$ to $10 ms$ to reach the postsynaptic neurone.
Excitatory neurotransmitters trigger ion channels to open and allow positive ions, such as sodium, to flow into the neurone.
Inhibitory neurotransmitters cause the membrane to become slightly depolarized by allowing sodium ions to enter.
Dopamine affects the brain synapses in the control of body movements and is linked to sensations of pleasure.
Inadequate production of dopamine is linked to schizophrenia, a disorder in which perception of reality is distorted.
Serotonin regulates temperature and sensory perception and is involved in mood control.
Endorphins act as natural painkillers in the brain and deficiency is linked to high blood pressure.
Norepinephrine complements the actions of the hormone epinephrine and readies the body to respond to danger.
The autonomic nervous system nerves either stimulate or inhibit the cardiac muscle but never the glands.
Sense organs are organs in which sensory receptors of a particular sense organ are highly concentrated.
The sclera is the transparent layer continuous with the cornea that refracts light rays.
Choroid contains a black pigment that absorbs light and prevents internal reflection of light within the eye.
The ciliary body is a round opening in the iris that allows light to enter the eye.
Retina consists of two types of photoreceptor cells called cone cells and rod cells.
Rod cells are responsible for colour vision under conditions of high light intensity.
The fovea is situated in line with the optical axis of the lens and provides the clearest vision.
The blind spot is sensitive to light because it contains a high concentration of rod cells.
Vitreous humour is a jelly-like substance that maintains the shape of the eye and prevents its collapse.
Aqueous humour is found in the vitreous chamber and prevents the collapse of the eyeball.
The suspensory ligament attaches the lens to the ciliary body and holds the lens vertically in the eye.
The optic nerve transmits nerve impulses from the brain to the retina to control vision.
Stereoscopic vision allows a person to judge the distance and depth of objects and estimate their speed.
In bright light, the radial muscles of the iris contract and the circular muscles relax.
Accommodation is the process of focusing on objects at different distances by changing the curvature of the lens.
When the eye is focused on a distant object, the ciliary muscles contract and the lens becomes more convex.
When the eye focuses on a nearby object, the ciliary muscles contract and the suspensory ligaments become slack.
The role of the iris is to control the curvature of the lens by changing the size of the pupil.
In dim light, the radial muscles contract and the circular muscles relax, dilating the pupil.
The cornea is the innermost coat of the eyeball and acts as a screen for the formation of images.
Endocrine glands secrete chemical messengers called hormones directly into the bloodstream.
The original Greek meaning of the word hormone is to inhibit or slow down motion.
Steroid hormones, such as testosterone, estrogen, and cortisol, are lipid-based compounds.
Water-soluble hormones include epinephrine, human growth hormone, and testosterone.
The hypothalamus secretes releasing and inhibiting hormones that regulate the anterior pituitary hormones.
Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) is secreted by the thyroid gland to produce thyroxine.
Human growth hormone (hGH) stimulates cell division, bone and muscle growth, and metabolic function.
Prolactin (PRL) is secreted by the posterior pituitary to stimulate milk production from mammary glands.
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) promotes the retention of water by the kidneys to regulate water balance.
Calcitonin is secreted by the parathyroid gland to raise blood calcium levels.
Thyroxine (T4) affects all tissues, increases metabolic rate, and regulates growth and development.
Glucocorticoides are fight-or-flight hormones secreted by the adrenal medulla.
The adrenal medulla secretes epinephrine and norepinephrine to coordinate the fight-or-flight response.
Insulin raises blood glucose levels by converting glycogen in the liver to glucose.
Glucagon raises blood glucose levels by converting glycogen in the liver to glucose.
Progesterone stimulates the uterine lining growth and promotes development of female secondary sexual characteristics.
Testosterone promotes sperm formation and the development of male secondary sexual characteristics.
In the nervous system, information is transmitted as a chemical substance known as a hormone.
The endocrine system transmission of information is typically slow and determined by the speed of blood flow.
Duration of effect in the nervous system is usually longer lasting, from minutes to days.
